speaker: CICERO
text: Good even, Casca. Brought you Caesar home?

speaker: CICERO
text: Why are you breathless? And why stare you so?

speaker: CASCA
text: Are not you moved, when all the sway of earth

speaker: CASCA
text: Shakes like a thing unfirm? O Cicero,

speaker: CASCA
text: I have seen tempests when the scolding winds

speaker: CASCA
text: Have rived the knotty oaks, and I have seen

speaker: CASCA
text: Th’ ambitious ocean swell and rage and foam

speaker: CASCA
text: To be exalted with the threat’ning clouds;

speaker: CASCA
text: But never till tonight, never till now,

speaker: CASCA
text: Did I go through a tempest dropping fire.

speaker: CASCA
text: Either there is a civil strife in heaven,

speaker: CASCA
text: Or else the world, too saucy with the gods,

speaker: CASCA
text: Incenses them to send destruction.

speaker: CICERO
text: Why, saw you anything more wonderful?

speaker: CASCA
text: A common slave (you know him well by sight)

speaker: CASCA
text: Held up his left hand, which did flame and burn

speaker: CASCA
text: Like twenty torches joined; and yet his hand,

speaker: CASCA
text: Not sensible of fire, remained unscorched.

speaker: CASCA
text: Besides (I ha’ not since put up my sword),

speaker: CASCA
text: Against the Capitol I met a lion,

speaker: CASCA
text: Who glazed upon me and went surly by

speaker: CASCA
text: Without annoying me. And there were drawn

speaker: CASCA
text: Upon a heap a hundred ghastly women,

speaker: CASCA
text: Transformèd with their fear, who swore they saw

speaker: CASCA
text: Men all in fire walk up and down the streets.

speaker: CASCA
text: And yesterday the bird of night did sit

speaker: CASCA
text: Even at noonday upon the marketplace,

speaker: CASCA
text: Hooting and shrieking. When these prodigies

speaker: CASCA
text: Do so conjointly meet, let not men say

speaker: CASCA
text: “These are their reasons, they are natural,”

speaker: CASCA
text: For I believe they are portentous things

speaker: CASCA
text: Unto the climate that they point upon.

speaker: CICERO
text: Indeed, it is a strange-disposèd time.

speaker: CICERO
text: But men may construe things after their fashion,

speaker: CICERO
text: Clean from the purpose of the things themselves.

speaker: CICERO
text: Comes Caesar to the Capitol tomorrow?

speaker: CASCA
text: He doth, for he did bid Antonius

speaker: CASCA
text: Send word to you he would be there tomorrow.

speaker: CICERO
text: Good night then, Casca. This disturbèd sky

speaker: CICERO
text: Is not to walk in.

speaker: CASCA
text: Farewell, Cicero

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Who’s there?

speaker: CASCA
text: A Roman.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Casca, by your voice.

speaker: CASCA
text: Your ear is good. Cassius, what night is this!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: A very pleasing night to honest men.

speaker: CASCA
text: Who ever knew the heavens menace so?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Those that have known the Earth so full of faults.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For my part, I have walked about the streets,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Submitting me unto the perilous night,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And thus unbracèd, Casca, as you see,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Have bared my bosom to the thunder-stone;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And when the cross blue lightning seemed to open

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The breast of heaven, I did present myself

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Even in the aim and very flash of it.

speaker: CASCA
text: But wherefore did you so much tempt the heavens?

speaker: CASCA
text: It is the part of men to fear and tremble

speaker: CASCA
text: When the most mighty gods by tokens send

speaker: CASCA
text: Such dreadful heralds to astonish us.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: You are dull, Casca, and those sparks of life

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That should be in a Roman you do want,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Or else you use not. You look pale, and gaze,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And put on fear, and cast yourself in wonder,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To see the strange impatience of the heavens.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But if you would consider the true cause

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why all these fires, why all these gliding ghosts,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why birds and beasts from quality and kind,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why old men, fools, and children calculate,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why all these things change from their ordinance,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Their natures, and preformèd faculties,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To monstrous quality—why, you shall find

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That heaven hath infused them with these spirits

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To make them instruments of fear and warning

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Unto some monstrous state.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Now could I, Casca, name to thee a man

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Most like this dreadful night,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That thunders, lightens, opens graves, and roars

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As doth the lion in the Capitol;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: A man no mightier than thyself or me

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In personal action, yet prodigious grown,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And fearful, as these strange eruptions are.

speaker: CASCA
text: ’Tis Caesar that you mean, is it not, Cassius?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Let it be who it is. For Romans now

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Have thews and limbs like to their ancestors.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But, woe the while, our fathers’ minds are dead,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And we are governed with our mothers’ spirits.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Our yoke and sufferance show us womanish.

speaker: CASCA
text: Indeed, they say the Senators tomorrow

speaker: CASCA
text: Mean to establish Caesar as a king,

speaker: CASCA
text: And he shall wear his crown by sea and land

speaker: CASCA
text: In every place save here in Italy.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I know where I will wear this dagger then;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Cassius from bondage will deliver Cassius.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Therein, you gods, you make the weak most strong;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Therein, you gods, you tyrants do defeat.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Nor stony tower, nor walls of beaten brass,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Nor airless dungeon, nor strong links of iron,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Can be retentive to the strength of spirit;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But life, being weary of these worldly bars,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Never lacks power to dismiss itself.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If I know this, know all the world besides,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That part of tyranny that I do bear

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I can shake off at pleasure.

speaker: CASCA
text: So can I.

speaker: CASCA
text: So every bondman in his own hand bears

speaker: CASCA
text: The power to cancel his captivity.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And why should Caesar be a tyrant, then?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Poor man, I know he would not be a wolf

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But that he sees the Romans are but sheep;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: He were no lion, were not Romans hinds.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Those that with haste will make a mighty fire

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Begin it with weak straws. What trash is Rome,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: What rubbish, and what offal when it serves

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For the base matter to illuminate

speaker: CASSIUS
text: So vile a thing as Caesar! But, O grief,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Where hast thou led me? I perhaps speak this

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Before a willing bondman; then, I know

speaker: CASSIUS
text: My answer must be made. But I am armed,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And dangers are to me indifferent.

speaker: CASCA
text: You speak to Casca, and to such a man

speaker: CASCA
text: That is no fleering telltale. Hold. My hand.

speaker: CASCA
text: Be factious for redress of all these griefs,

speaker: CASCA
text: And I will set this foot of mine as far

speaker: CASCA
text: As who goes farthest.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: There’s a bargain made.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Now know you, Casca, I have moved already

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Some certain of the noblest-minded Romans

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To undergo with me an enterprise

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Of honorable-dangerous consequence.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And I do know by this they stay for me

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In Pompey’s Porch. For now, this fearful night,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: There is no stir or walking in the streets;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And the complexion of the element

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In favor ’s like the work we have in hand,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Most bloody, fiery, and most terrible.

speaker: CASCA
text: Stand close awhile, for here comes one in haste.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: ’Tis Cinna; I do know him by his gait.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: He is a friend.—Cinna, where haste you so?

speaker: CINNA
text: To find out you. Who’s that? Metellus Cimber?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: No, it is Casca, one incorporate

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To our attempts. Am I not stayed for, Cinna?

speaker: CINNA
text: I am glad on ’t. What a fearful night is this!

speaker: CINNA
text: There’s two or three of us have seen strange sights.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Am I not stayed for? Tell me.

speaker: CINNA
text: Yes, you are. O Cassius, if you could

speaker: CINNA
text: But win the noble Brutus to our party—

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Be you content. Good Cinna, take this paper,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And look you lay it in the Praetor’s chair,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Where Brutus may but find it; and throw this

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In at his window; set this up with wax

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Upon old Brutus’ statue. All this done,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Repair to Pompey’s Porch, where you shall find us.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Is Decius Brutus and Trebonius there?

speaker: CINNA
text: All but Metellus Cimber, and he’s gone

speaker: CINNA
text: To seek you at your house. Well, I will hie

speaker: CINNA
text: And so bestow these papers as you bade me.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That done, repair to Pompey’s Theater.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Come, Casca, you and I will yet ere day

speaker: CASSIUS
text: See Brutus at his house. Three parts of him

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Is ours already, and the man entire

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Upon the next encounter yields him ours.

speaker: CASCA
text: O, he sits high in all the people’s hearts,

speaker: CASCA
text: And that which would appear offense in us

speaker: CASCA
text: His countenance, like richest alchemy,

speaker: CASCA
text: Will change to virtue and to worthiness.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Him and his worth and our great need of him

speaker: CASSIUS
text: You have right well conceited. Let us go,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For it is after midnight, and ere day

speaker: CASSIUS
text: We will awake him and be sure of him.
